The word “apostle” signifies messenger; they were Christ's messengers, sent forth to proclaim his kingdom. Christ gave them power to heal every kind of sickness. In the grace of the gospel there is a remedy for every affliction. There is no spiritual disease, but there is power in Christ to cure it. Their names are recorded, and that is their honor; yet they had more reason to rejoice that their names were written in heaven, while the important and powerful names of great people on earth are buried in the dust.

The Gentiles must not have the gospel brought to them until the Jews have refused it. This restriction was only for the apostles’ first mission. Wherever they went they must proclaim, “The kingdom of heaven is at hand.” They preached to establish faith; the kingdom to inspire hope; of heaven, to inspire love for heavenly things and contempt for earthly things; which is at hand, so that people may prepare for it without delay. Christ gave power to work miracles to confirm their teaching. This is not necessary now that the kingdom of God has come. It showed that the intent of the teaching they preached was to heal sick souls and to raise those who were dead in sin. In proclaiming the gospel of free grace for the healing and saving of people’s souls, we must above all avoid the appearance of a hired worker's attitude. They were directed what to do in unfamiliar towns and cities. The servant of Christ is an ambassador of peace wherever he is sent. His message is even to the most sinful people, but it is important that he finds the best people in every place. It is our duty to pray earnestly for all and to treat everyone with courtesy. They were directed how to act toward those who rejected them. The entire counsel of God must be declared, and those who will not pay attention to the gracious message must be shown that their situation is dangerous. This should be seriously considered by all who hear the gospel, lest their privileges only serve to increase their condemnation.

Our Lord warned his disciples to prepare for persecution. They were to avoid anything that gave an advantage to their enemies, any involvement in worldly or political matters, any appearance of evil or selfishness, and any underhanded tactics. Christ foretold troubles not only so that the troubles would not be a surprise, but so that they might confirm their faith. He tells them what they will suffer and from whom. Thus Christ has dealt fairly and faithfully with us by telling us the worst we can meet in his service; and he wants us to deal with ourselves in the same way, by considering the cost. Persecutors are worse than beasts because they prey on those of their own kind. The strongest bonds of love and duty have often been broken because of hatred for Christ. Suffering from friends and relatives is very painful—nothing cuts more deeply. It appears plainly that all who want to live a godly life in Christ Jesus must suffer persecution; and we must expect to enter the kingdom of God through many trials. Along with these predictions of trouble are guidance and comfort for times of trial. The disciples of Christ are hated and persecuted like snakes, their destruction is sought, and they need a snake’s wisdom. Be as harmless as doves. Not only do no harm to anyone, but bear no ill will toward anyone. There must be careful planning, but not anxious or confusing thoughts; let this care be given to God. The disciples of Christ should focus more on doing what is right than on speaking well. In case of great danger, the disciples of Christ may go out of the way of danger, though they must not go out of the way of duty. No sinful, unlawful means may be used to escape; for then it is not an opportunity provided by God. The fear of people brings a trap—a confusing trap that disturbs our peace; an ensnaring trap by which we are drawn into sin; therefore, we must strive against and pray against it. Tribulation, distress, and persecution cannot take away God's love for them or their love for him. Fear Him who is able to destroy both soul and body in hell. They must deliver their message publicly, because everyone is deeply concerned with the teaching of the gospel. The entire counsel of God must be made known. Christ shows them why they should be cheerful. Their sufferings testify against those who oppose his gospel. When God calls us to speak for him, we may depend on him to teach us what to say. A hopeful expectation of the end of our troubles will greatly support us under them. They may be brought through to the end because the sufferers will be supported by him. Strength will be available for each day. And it is great encouragement to those who are doing Christ's work that it is a work which will certainly be accomplished. See how the care of God’s provision extends to all creatures, even to sparrows. This should silence all the fears of God's people; You are of more value than many sparrows. And even the hairs of your head are all numbered. This shows the account that God takes and keeps of his people. It is our duty not only to believe in Christ but to profess that faith, by suffering for him when we are called to it, as well as in serving him. That denial of Christ only is meant which is persistent, and that confession only can have the blessed reward promised here, which is the true and constant expression of faith and love. Religion is worth everything; all who believe its truth will pay the price and make everything else yield to it. Christ will lead us through suffering to glory with him. Those are best prepared for the life to come who are least attached to this present life. Though kindness done to Christ's disciples be ever so small, yet if there is occasion for it, and ability to do no more, it shall be accepted. Christ does not say that they deserve a reward; for we cannot merit anything from God’s hand; but they will receive a reward as a free gift from God. Let us boldly confess Christ and show love to him in all things.
